content: Applied in back to front order

`BlendFunc(GL_ONE, GL_ONE_MINUS_SRC_ALPHA)`
So `source RGBA + (existing framebuffer RGBA) * (1.0 - source alpha)`

referenced_message.content:
1) Hard, I'd rather try to refit the existing one
2) Should be already possible. With that old branch I mentioned yesterday it should be also possible to use different animation parameters for front and other textures (currently animParams are applied for all textures of particular effect)
3) Very hard, do post the blending formulas though. Blending is a fixed function of hardware, there's a little wiggle room in the shader, like premultiplying something by something or do other primitive tricks. In this case we need explicit blending modes (formulas) to figure if they can coexist in the shader.
4) Probably not hard

content:
1) Hard, I'd rather try to refit the existing one
2) Should be already possible. With that old branch I mentioned yesterday it should be also possible to use different animation parameters for front and other textures (currently animParams are applied for all textures of particular effect)
3) Very hard, do post the blending formulas though. Blending is a fixed function of hardware, there's a little wiggle room in the shader, like premultiplying something by something or do other primitive tricks. In this case we need explicit blending modes (formulas) to figure if they can coexist in the shader.
4) Probably not hard

referenced_message.content:
In nature, even things like dropping a pebble in  water ends up yielding a very uniformal force output. So with nuclear explosions, that involve heat etc the outburst will very much maintain an almost mathematical conformity to radius and circumference. The heat and fire will dissipate much sooner than the blast wave, as much of that is also just sonic boom after sonic boom, which inevitably carries with it more distruction, dust and interference. The smoke is also heated , so in the example on the right youll never actually find the dust cloud outpacing the the shockwave so dramatically. The initial fireball is almost instant, the blast wave very high velocity smoke fire etc(image left), then shockwave almost subtle and transparent keeps traversing and slows in speed until it melts and fades into the ground as it loses energy and the smoke will rise behind it. This is where wind and stuff makes it move beyond its intial force vectors. 

Smoke cant exactly jolt forward like that as if it wasnt wearing a seatbelt XD. So to speak... But this is awesome watching all these iterations.

content: *Connection attempt for FalZeeN* *Catching up*
Grass Shading Thread

In nature, even things like dropping a pebble in  water ends up yielding a very uniformal force output. So with nuclear explosions, that involve heat etc the outburst will very much maintain an almost mathematical conformity to radius and circumference. The heat and fire will dissipate much sooner than the blast wave, as much of that is also just sonic boom after sonic boom, which inevitably carries with it more distruction, dust and interference. The smoke is also heated , so in the example on the right youll never actually find the dust cloud outpacing the the shockwave so dramatically. The initial fireball is almost instant, the blast wave very high velocity smoke fire etc(image left), then shockwave almost subtle and transparent keeps traversing and slows in speed until it melts and fades into the ground as it loses energy and the smoke will rise behind it. This is where wind and stuff makes it move beyond its intial force vectors. 

Smoke cant exactly jolt forward like that as if it wasnt wearing a seatbelt XD. So to speak... But this is awesome watching all these iterations.
PS.. just realized we're almost over 500 unique particle GFX - some consisting of 15 separate particle generators each 🤯
its a very nice effect!

I think I like better5 relatively the best.

- The smoke distort/dissipate suddenly starts at the end, which feels weird.
- The noise/roughness should be in the explosion from the start imho, and then slowly fade out.
- The smoke/clouds should settle down/slow down at the end, not speed up.

I think it doesn't need to be too distorted - the circly-shape does help convey the shockwave.
But maybe 2-3 variants with more/less distortion of the circleshape is good to test

message.content:
## Current Ideas Cook:

**Laser Cannon Types Post Standardisation**
- Light Energy Blaster: Balance TBA, modeled on commando blaster
- Heavy Energy Blaster: Balance TBA, modeled on cataphract cannon
- Machine guns: Are currently being reworked, will be their own balance and look.

**Seaplane gunships:**
- Replace sabre laser cannon with an a2g lightning cannon. Leaning into making the unit capable of leak defense. 
- Replace the Cutlass burst plasma with a laser cannon. Leaning into more laser cannons and fast projectiles for cortex.
- Legion seaplane gunship panned to be an A10 style unit, bombing with a machine gun 

~~**Titan**
- Replace the laser cannons with plasma, leaning into the crowd control aspect to go with the single target tachyon~~

**Bulwark**
- Replace the tachyon with a laser cannon... Why does this unit have just a worse tachyon? Laser cannon can have good AOE and faster fire rate. Similar change to the cataphract remaster

**Salamander**
- This unit could be a candidate for having a laser cannon. More leaning into just a regular beam laser to replace the heatray.

referenced_message.content:
I think at minimum you'll need for a VFX system;

- UV mapped meshes in the particle system
- ability to pan textures over time on a mesh or billboard
- flipbook animation for mesh or billboard
- multiple mixed meshes/billboards possible in the same effect
- slightly complex material texture maps like albedo, normal, specular emissive and opacity maps 
- different shader types (lit, unlit, alpha masked, alpha blended, two sided or backface culling)
- ability to have billboards have different facing modes, like camera facing or a specific vector (facing up, for example)

One of the important gameplay parts of explosion are, to not obscure too much.
Mushroom clouds kinda are on the other side of the spectrum here.
Having 1 or even 2-3 nuke explosions should not hide that part of the map for too long.

Also there is the satisfaction of seeing everything explode.
These secondary explosions of units/buildings is pretty much that, and using the 'default':

1 - Bright white orb (can't see nothing)
2 - Than the slow rise of a big White-Orange donut/orb
3 - The added stem moving up with that

These would likely completely obscure everything beneath it.

I think it will be very hard to impossible to not loose this with the traditional mushroom-nuke-explo.
Or you'd have to make it semi-transparant or be very short/quick - which likely will really hurt the mush-room effect you're players probably expect.

(you'd get that short nuke explo like in C&C1 )

## Another thing is Anti-nuked-nukes:

You also don't want these to have mushrooms, as these will obscure A Lot more, and usually above the mid of the map.
So we'd effectively would need 2 explosions, that A. look similar-ish but where we can remove things from the anti-nuked shots so they don't blind all players.
